artifacts.name: Outdoor Athlete
artifacts.description: Person who regularly performs outdoor activities (cycling, running) and wants to track performance over time
responsibilities: Review distance, time, and elevation metrics
Compare performance across routes and time periods

artifacts.name: Life Planner
artifacts.description: Person who organizes personal tasks, goals, and projects across multiple life domains
responsibilities: Create and organize tasks within domains and areas
Prioritize and schedule work across projects

artifacts.name: Data Curator
artifacts.description: Person who imports, cleans, and manages activity data from multiple sources
responsibilities: Import activities from Cyclemeter, Strava, and Wahoo
Export data for sharing or backup

artifacts.name: Solo Athlete
artifacts.description: Primary user who tracks personal outdoor activities — cycling and running — and reviews performance trends. Uses Darwin as a single-player tool.
goals: See all activities on a map with route details
Track distance, time, and elevation trends over weeks/months/years
Import activities from device apps without manual data entry
frustrations: Strava and Cyclemeter each show partial views of activity history
Switching between apps to get a complete picture
Losing GPS data when switching platforms

artifacts.name: Task Organizer
artifacts.description: User who manages personal tasks and recurring responsibilities across life domains (fitness, home, work). Uses Darwin alongside the activity tracker.
goals: Organize tasks hierarchically by domain and area
Track task completion on a calendar
Manage recurring tasks without re-creating them manually
frustrations: Generic to-do apps lack domain/area hierarchy
Calendar apps show time but not task structure
No single tool connects activity tracking with task planning

artifacts.name: Goal Tracker
artifacts.description: User who sets long-term goals and tracks progress via a priority roadmap organized into projects and categories.
goals: Visualize project priorities in a Kanban-style board
Track priority status transitions (open, deferred, completed)
Schedule and review priorities on the calendar
frustrations: Project management tools are too heavyweight for personal use
Hard to connect daily tasks with long-term priorities
Status changes get lost without a visual board

artifacts.label: Multi-source activity import
artifacts.statement: Users must be able to import activities from Cyclemeter (.db, KML, GPX), Strava (OAuth API), Wahoo (FIT), and MTBProject (GPX) without manual data entry
artifacts.source: Core feature — multiple import pipelines exist
artifacts.rationale: Users switch between tracking apps and need a unified history

artifacts.label: Multi-view activity browsing
artifacts.statement: Users must be able to view activities as cards, a sortable data table, or trend charts — all sharing the same filter state
artifacts.rationale: Different views serve different goals: quick scan (cards), detailed comparison (table), pattern discovery (trends)

artifacts.label: Trend drill-down
artifacts.statement: Users should be able to click a yearly bar to drill into monthly, then weekly, filtering the visible activities accordingly
artifacts.rationale: Top-down exploration lets users discover patterns without manually setting date ranges

artifacts.label: Composable activity filters
artifacts.statement: Users must be able to filter activities by saved view criteria, time range, route, and partner — filters compose additively
artifacts.rationale: Power users need precise slicing across multiple dimensions simultaneously

artifacts.label: Bulk activity operations
artifacts.statement: Users should be able to select multiple activities and bulk-edit route assignments, add/remove partners, or delete
artifacts.rationale: After large imports, batch cleanup is essential — editing one-by-one is prohibitive

artifacts.label: Hierarchical task organization
artifacts.statement: Users must be able to organize tasks in a Domain > Area > Task hierarchy with drag-and-drop reordering
artifacts.rationale: Flat to-do lists fail for multi-domain life management — hierarchy provides structure without rigidity

artifacts.label: Recurring task definitions
artifacts.statement: Users should be able to define recurring task templates that spawn instances into designated areas
artifacts.rationale: Weekly/monthly responsibilities should not require manual re-creation

artifacts.label: Calendar task integration
artifacts.statement: Users must be able to view tasks, activities, and priorities on a unified calendar with drag-to-reschedule
artifacts.rationale: Time-based views connect daily execution with weekly/monthly planning

artifacts.label: Priority roadmap board
artifacts.statement: Users should be able to manage priorities in a Project > Category > Priority hierarchy with Kanban-style drag-and-drop
artifacts.rationale: Personal projects need lightweight goal tracking without heavyweight PM tooling

artifacts.label: Timezone-aware date handling
artifacts.statement: All dates must be stored as UTC and displayed in the user profile timezone
artifacts.rationale: Activity timestamps from devices are UTC — displaying in local time prevents confusion

artifacts.label: Offline data access
artifacts.statement: Users could access activity data offline via client-side SQLite (sql.js)
artifacts.rationale: Reviewing past activities during outdoor trips without connectivity would be valuable

artifacts.label: KML export
artifacts.statement: Users should be able to export activities as KML files for use in Google Earth and sharing
artifacts.rationale: Users want to share routes visually and archive GPS data in a standard format

artifacts.name: Import a morning bike ride
artifacts.narrative: After a morning cycling session, the user opens Darwin, navigates to Maps > Import, drops their Cyclemeter .db file onto the import area. Darwin auto-detects the format, shows a preview of 1 new activity with route name and GPS coordinates. The user confirms and saves. The activity appears in the Cards view with distance, time, and elevation. The user clicks it to see the GPS route on the map.
preconditions: Cyclemeter .db file is available on local disk
postconditions: Activity visible in Cards, Table, and Trends views
Route created or matched to existing route

frames.caption: User opens Darwin after a morning ride
frames.systemResponse: Import wizard appears with file drop zone
frames.emotionalState: Motivated — wants to see the ride data

frames.caption: User drops Cyclemeter file
frames.systemResponse: Auto-detects Cyclemeter DB format, shows format badge

frames.caption: Preview shows the ride
frames.userAction: Reviews preview: 1 activity, route name, 2400 coordinates
frames.systemResponse: Preview panel populates with activity summary

frames.caption: User saves the import
frames.systemResponse: Progress bar fills, success toast appears

frames.caption: User views the ride on the map
frames.systemResponse: Route detail view shows GPS track on Leaflet map with elevation profile
frames.emotionalState: Gratified — complete picture of the ride

artifacts.name: Analyze yearly cycling trends
artifacts.narrative: The user opens Maps and switches to the Trends view. They see yearly distance bars for the past several years. They click the 2025 bar to drill into monthly view, noticing a dip in March. They click March to see weekly detail, then switch to the Table view to see which routes they rode that month. They filter by a specific route to compare times across visits.
preconditions: Multiple years of imported activities exist
postconditions: User has identified a performance pattern
Filters are set to a specific route and time range

frames.caption: User opens Trends view
frames.userAction: Navigate to Maps, click Trends toggle
frames.systemResponse: Yearly distance bar chart renders with all-time data

frames.caption: User spots an interesting year
frames.systemResponse: Chart drills into monthly view for 2025, filters update

frames.caption: User notices a low month
frames.systemResponse: Chart drills into weekly view for March 2025

frames.caption: User switches to Table view
frames.systemResponse: DataGrid shows only March 2025 activities (filters preserved)

frames.caption: User filters by a specific route
frames.userAction: Opens route picker, selects a favorite route
frames.systemResponse: Table narrows to that route in March 2025

artifacts.name: Plan the week with tasks and calendar
artifacts.setting: Home, desktop or tablet, weekly planning session
artifacts.narrative: On Sunday evening, the user opens Tasks and reviews their domains (Fitness, Home, Work). They create a few new tasks in the Fitness area, mark one as priority, and drag-reorder them. They switch to the Calendar view and toggle Tasks mode to see the week ahead. They drag a completed task to today to record when it was done. They also toggle Activities to see rides scheduled from Strava.
preconditions: User has domains and areas configured

frames.caption: User opens Tasks on Sunday evening
frames.systemResponse: Domain tabs load, Fitness domain active, area cards with tasks visible

frames.caption: User adds new tasks
frames.userAction: Types task description in Fitness > Cycling area, presses enter
frames.systemResponse: Task appears at bottom of area card

frames.caption: User prioritizes and reorders
frames.userAction: Toggles priority flag, drags task to top of list
frames.systemResponse: Task highlighted as priority, list reorders with animation

frames.caption: User switches to Calendar
frames.systemResponse: Month view shows tasks, activities, and priorities on the timeline

frames.caption: User records a completed task
frames.userAction: Drags a completed task to today on the calendar
frames.systemResponse: Task marked done, done_ts set to today, green highlight

artifacts.name: Browse ride photos
artifacts.setting: Home, desktop Mac with local photos
artifacts.narrative: After importing a ride with geotagged photos on their Mac, the user navigates to the activity detail and taps the photo icon. The photo browser opens showing thumbnails filtered to the ride time window. They expand the time buffer to +1 hour to catch post-ride photos. They click a thumbnail to open the lightbox and swipe through full-size images.
preconditions: Photo scanning is enabled in settings
Photos exist on local filesystem within the ride time window
postconditions: User has viewed photos associated with the ride

frames.caption: User opens a recent ride detail
frames.userAction: Navigate to Maps, click a ride card
frames.systemResponse: Route detail view shows GPS track, stats, and photo icon
frames.emotionalState: Nostalgic — wants to revisit the ride

frames.caption: User opens the photo browser
frames.systemResponse: Thumbnail grid loads with photos from the ride time window

frames.caption: User expands the time buffer
frames.userAction: Adjusts time filter to +1 hour after ride
frames.systemResponse: Additional post-ride photos appear in the grid
frames.emotionalState: Pleased — catches the coffee stop photos

frames.caption: User views full-size photos
frames.systemResponse: Lightbox opens with zoom and swipe navigation

artifacts.name: Manage project priorities on the roadmap
artifacts.narrative: The user opens Swarm and selects their personal project. They see categories as columns with priority cards. They create a new priority in the Must-Have column, give it a title and description. Later, after deciding to delay it, they change its status to Deferred. At month-end review, they mark completed priorities and check the calendar to see scheduled ones.
preconditions: At least one project with categories exists

frames.caption: User opens Swarm roadmap
frames.systemResponse: Project tabs load, Kanban columns show categories with priority cards

frames.caption: User creates a new priority
frames.userAction: Clicks + in Must-Have column, enters title and description
frames.systemResponse: New priority card appears in the column

frames.caption: User defers a priority
frames.userAction: Opens a priority card, changes status to Deferred
frames.systemResponse: Card moves to deferred visual state (yellow chip)

frames.caption: User reviews completed priorities
frames.systemResponse: Completed cards appear (green chips) alongside open ones

frames.caption: User checks calendar for scheduled priorities
frames.userAction: Navigates to Calendar, toggles Priorities mode
frames.systemResponse: Scheduled priorities appear on their target dates

artifacts.title: Three-pillar architecture
artifacts.body: Darwin is organized into three toggleable app pillars (controlled by profile flags app_tasks, app_maps, app_swarm): (1) Maps — activity tracking, import, visualization, photos; (2) Tasks — hierarchical domain/area/task planning with recurring tasks; (3) Swarm — project/category/priority roadmap. Each pillar has independent nav items and can be enabled/disabled per user.

artifacts.title: Client-side filtering strategy
artifacts.body: All activity filtering (view criteria, time range, route, partner) happens client-side in JavaScript after fetching the full dataset. This works for a personal app with thousands of activities but would not scale to multi-user. Saved views store criteria as JSON and are evaluated with a custom filter engine (mapViewFilter.js).

artifacts.title: Photo system is macOS-only
artifacts.body: The photo browser depends on a local filesystem proxy to scan and serve geotagged photos from the Mac Photos library or file system. Photo metadata is indexed in IndexedDB. This feature is not available on other platforms and requires the proxy to be running.

artifacts.title: Auth token lifecycle
artifacts.body: Auth uses Amazon Cognito with SRP. Refresh tokens are stored in HttpOnly cookies (90-day TTL). ID and access tokens are kept in memory only — lost on page refresh, then silently re-acquired via refresh token. Auto-refresh is scheduled 5 minutes before token expiry.

artifacts.name: Single-player simplicity
artifacts.statement: Darwin is a personal tool — every feature should optimize for a single user, not multi-tenancy
artifacts.description: Avoid complexity that only serves collaborative or enterprise use cases. Client-side filtering, local storage, and in-memory state are acceptable trade-offs.
artifacts.source: Architecture review — personal app with single Cognito user
examples: Client-side filtering instead of server-side pagination
In-memory auth tokens instead of server session management
counterExamples: Server-side query optimization for concurrent users

artifacts.name: Progressive disclosure for complexity
artifacts.statement: Surface simple defaults first; reveal advanced options only when the user seeks them
artifacts.description: Darwin has deep functionality (import filters, trend drill-downs, bulk operations) but the entry point for each feature should be immediately usable without configuration.
artifacts.source: Import wizard has an Advanced toggle; Trends default to yearly distance
examples: Import wizard auto-detects format and shows a preview before asking for filter config
Trends view defaults to yearly distance bars — no setup needed
Calendar defaults to current month with tasks visible
counterExamples: Requiring users to configure filters before seeing any data
Showing all import options (minDelta, precision) by default
Displaying every filter dimension simultaneously on Maps

artifacts.name: Multi-view, shared state
artifacts.statement: The same data should be explorable through multiple views (cards, table, charts, calendar, map) that share filter and selection state
artifacts.description: Users think about their data differently at different times. Switching between views should feel like rotating the same object, not navigating to a different page.
artifacts.source: Maps page three-view architecture (Cards, Table, Trends)
examples: Clicking a Trends bar filters the Table/Cards views to that time range
Route and partner filters persist across Cards, Table, and Trends
Calendar shows tasks, activities, and priorities from different data sources on one timeline
counterExamples: Each view having its own independent filter state
Losing context when switching between Cards and Table
Requiring users to re-enter filters after changing views

artifacts.name: Offline-ready architecture
artifacts.statement: Core features should work without a persistent server connection, using client-side data stores
artifacts.description: As a personal activity tracker for outdoor enthusiasts, Darwin should support reviewing past data when connectivity is unavailable. sql.js and IndexedDB provide the foundation.
examples: Browsing previously loaded activities from client-side SQLite
Viewing cached photo thumbnails from IndexedDB
Accessing task lists from local storage
counterExamples: Requiring server roundtrip to view any activity data
Blocking the UI while waiting for API responses to render previously-seen data
Forcing re-authentication to access cached data

artifacts.name: Drag-and-drop as primary interaction
artifacts.statement: Spatial manipulation (drag-to-reorder, drag-to-reschedule, drag-between-containers) should be the primary way to organize and prioritize
artifacts.description: Task ordering, domain tabs, calendar rescheduling, and priority management all use DnD. This provides a tactile, low-friction interaction that scales to touch devices.
artifacts.source: React-DND and Hello-Pangea/DND usage across Tasks, Swarm, Calendar
examples: Dragging tasks between areas to reorganize
Dragging priority cards between Kanban columns
counterExamples: Using only form fields or dropdowns to change task ordering
Requiring a separate edit dialog to move a task to a different area

artifacts.title: Import format detection is opaque
artifacts.description: The import wizard auto-detects file format but provides no visual feedback about which format was detected or why. Users who have files from multiple sources may not understand what processing will occur.
artifacts.evidence: CyclemeterImport component detects format silently — only the preview reveals what was parsed
artifacts.recommendation: Show a detected-format badge (e.g., Cyclemeter DB, Wahoo FIT, Strava GPX) immediately after file drop, with a brief description of what will be extracted

artifacts.title: Filter state is not visually persistent
artifacts.description: When multiple filters are active on the Maps page (view + time + route + partner), there is no consolidated indicator showing all active filters. Users may forget they have filters applied and see unexpectedly narrow results.
artifacts.evidence: Each filter is set via separate dialogs/interactions — no unified filter pill bar
artifacts.recommendation: Add a persistent filter chip bar below the view toggle showing all active filters with dismiss buttons

artifacts.title: Photo browser is platform-locked to macOS
artifacts.description: The photo browsing feature depends on a local filesystem proxy that only works on macOS. There is no fallback, degraded mode, or clear messaging for non-Mac users.
artifacts.evidence: Photo settings page checks proxy availability; no cross-platform alternative exists
artifacts.recommendation: Either implement a web-based photo upload flow as a cross-platform fallback, or clearly communicate the macOS requirement in the UI and hide photo features on other platforms

artifacts.title: Hierarchy management is scattered across pages
artifacts.description: Editing domains, areas, projects, and categories requires navigating to 4 separate management pages (/areaedit, /domainedit, /projectedit, /categoryedit). The mental model connecting these entities is not reinforced by the navigation.
artifacts.evidence: Each edit page is a standalone route with its own layout pattern
artifacts.recommendation: Consider a unified tree/outline editor that shows the full hierarchy (Domain > Area for tasks; Project > Category for roadmap) in a single view with inline editing

artifacts.title: Drag-and-drop works well across all task interactions
artifacts.description: The drag-and-drop implementation for tasks, domains, calendar events, and priority cards is consistently implemented and touch-friendly. It provides a cohesive interaction pattern across the app.
artifacts.evidence: React-DND and Hello-Pangea/DND are used consistently with touch backend support
artifacts.recommendation: Continue this pattern for any new organizational features

artifacts.title: Trend drill-down is intuitive
artifacts.description: The yearly > monthly > weekly drill-down in the Trends view works naturally — clicking a bar narrows the time range and increases granularity. The Clear Filter button provides an obvious escape hatch.
artifacts.evidence: TrendsStore drill-down mapping with DRILL_DOWN constant; Clear Filter resets all state
artifacts.recommendation: Consider extending drill-down to other dimensions (e.g., click a route name to filter by route)

artifacts.name: Maps Hub
artifacts.description: Central activity browsing screen with three view modes and composable filters
elements: View toggle bar (Cards | Table | Trends)
Filter toolbar (View selector, Route picker, Partner picker, Clear)
Activity count + route count summary
Cards: paginated grid of activity summary cards
Table: sortable DataGrid with bulk selection
Trends: Recharts bar/line with metric and timeframe toggles
Floating action button (Import, Export, Settings)
annotations: Filters compose — each narrows the shared dataset
Clicking a Trends bar sets a time filter visible in Cards/Table
Bulk operations (edit route, add partner, delete) available in Table view

artifacts.name: Task Planner
artifacts.description: Hierarchical task organization with draggable domain tabs and area cards
elements: Domain tab bar (draggable tabs, + button for new domain)
Area cards grid within active domain
Task list within each area card (checkbox, description, priority flag)
Drag handles on tasks for reordering
Priority card overlay toggle per domain
New task input at bottom of each area card
Menu button per area (rename, close, delete)
annotations: Tasks can be dragged between areas
Done checkbox sets done_ts for calendar integration

artifacts.name: Import Wizard
artifacts.description: Multi-format activity import with auto-detection, filtering, and preview
elements: File drop zone with format detection badge
Format indicator (Cyclemeter DB, Wahoo FIT, Strava GPX, etc.)
Filter mode selector (All Routes, Route IDs, Notes Like, Date Range)
Advanced config toggle (minDelta, precision)
Preview panel (activity count, route count, coordinate count)
Save button with progress indicator
Strava OAuth connect button (alternative flow)
annotations: Filters narrow which activities from the file are imported
Preview shows what will be saved before committing

artifacts.name: Calendar View
artifacts.description: Unified calendar showing tasks, activities, and priorities with drag-to-reschedule
elements: View type toggle (Day, Week, Month)
Date navigation (prev, next, today)
Event mode toggles (Tasks, Activities, Priorities)
Day cells with color-coded event chips
Period summary panel (task completion %, activity totals, priority stats)
Mobile: vertical scroll with IntersectionObserver lazy loading
annotations: Activity events link to route detail

artifacts.name: Swarm Roadmap
artifacts.description: Kanban-style priority roadmap organized by project and category
elements: Project tab bar (draggable, + button)
Category columns within active project
Priority cards within each column (title, status chip, scheduled date)
Status filter bar (open, deferred, completed)
Drag handles for reordering cards between categories
New priority button in column header
Project/category management menu
annotations: Click card to navigate to PriorityDetail view

extensions.description: Personal activity tracker and life-management web app — interactive maps, activity import (Cyclemeter/Strava/Wahoo), photo browsing, calendar views, trend charts, hierarchical task planning, and priority roadmaps
